title: Home

content:
Background Knowledge#This writeup is for Cupids Matchmaker, a room in TryHackMe (THM) for Valentine’s 2026 Event. This room focuses on XSS Vulnerability.
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) is a condition when a web application accepts untrusted data from a user and sends it to a web browser without proper sanitazion. This allows an attacker to inject and execute malicious scripts (usually JavaScript) within the victim’s website. There are 3 main types of XXS. They are Stored, Reflected, and Blind XSS.
XSS Types: Reflected – The script is included in a request and is immediately reflected back in the response. Stored – The script is stored on the server and executes every time a user accesses the page. DOM – The script is executed as a result of modifying the Document Object Model(DOM) environment. More towards the client-side code.
More information on XSS can be found through the OWASP official website https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/xss/ .
Walkthrough#First things first, reccoinassance. I ran an nmap scan on the machine, but it didn’t reveal anything valuable. From there, I moved on to browsing the website. One detail really stood out on their landing page, the site mentioned that every match is carefully curated by real people. The fact they emphasized manual admin reviews gave me a strong lead on a potential attack vector.
To confirm my suspicions, I performed a directory search and found an admin login page.
I tried some basic checks on the admin portal. Simple password brute-forcing didn’t work, and there were no obvious hints provided. This solidified my attack vector, to use an XSS attack, specifically Blind XSS, to steal the admin’s session cookie.
Since I didn’t know exactly which input field was vulnerable to unsanitized data, I tested multiple payloads across all available inputs. Eventually, one of them(the one i screenshotted) successfully triggered a callback.
After setting up my listener, I waited for the admin bot to review the survey. Once the bot opened it, I received a callback containing the admin's cookie, which served as the flag.
summary:

This allows an attacker to inject and execute malicious scripts (usually JavaScript) within the victim’s website. There are 3 main types of XXS. They are Stored, Reflected, and Blind XSS.
title: THM: Cupids Matchmaker Writeup

content:
Fun FactThis is my first Writeup on my personal website. Hope you enjoy!
Background Knowledge#This writeup is for TryHeartMe, a room in TryHackMe (THM) for Valentine’s 2026 Event. This room focuses on JWT manupulation.
A JWT (JSON Web Token) is a cryptographic signature specifically generated for each website visitor. It helps the website remember who you are and is typically stored in the cookie for authentication, authorization and session management. A standard JWT token consist of 3 parts. They are header, Payload and Signature.
Header: alg – the cryptographic algorithm used to sign the token (HMAC with SHA-256 or RSA with SHA-256). typ – the type of the token, which is usually “JWT”.
Payload: exp - expiration time of token before the token becomes invalid. iat - issued at time. iss - the issuer of the token. sub - the subject, which is often the user ID. others - anything else the developers wants to put in.
Signature: signature - Base64-encoded header and payload + secret key is signed with the algorithm specified in the header.
You can experiment with decoding and modifying your own JWTs on their official website https://www.jwt.io/.
Walkthrough#The first thing to do when approaching a target machine is reconnaissance. Always look around the target and check functionalities or features that might be useful for your attack later on. I started by creating an account, logging in, attempting to make a purchase, and clicking on all the products in the shop to understand how the website operates.
After exploring the website, I found something interesting in Burp Suite while checking the network requests. The application set a cookie named tryheartme_jwt. This serves as a massive clue, as standard session cookies do not usually have such a specific, JWT-related naming convention. It seemed like the developers were pointing directly toward a JWT manipulation path.
I decided to decode that token and discovered that the developer stored the user’s role and credits directly inside the JWT payload. This is a possible attack vector to persue since there are two critical variables in the payload(role and credits). So I decided to try modifying both.
My thought process was simple: I am currently a standard user, so the first step is to elevate my privileges. This might allow me to see the valenflag product, the hidden item. I guessed the role admin because it is a common naming convention developers use for elevated access. For the credits, it is just a matter of changing the integer value higher so I can afford the items.
Since I want to find the hidden product, the first place to test this is by reloading the website while injecting my newly forged admin token.
And voila, it worked! Turns out the signature verification is completely missing. The backend only checks the payload contents without verifying the cryptographic signature. Now that the system recognizes me as staff (admin), I can finally see the valenflag. Let’s try to buy it.
Do not forget, though: after clicking on the product, you have to intercept and modify the token again because the forged token is not permanently saved in your browser.
Now, simply repeat the process of replacing the original token with your forged admin token every time you perform an action on the website, such as pressing the buy button.After completing the buying process, the website finally generates a receipt. If you look closely at that receipt, you will find your flag!
summary: Fun FactThis is my first Writeup on my personal website. Hope you enjoy!
title: THM: TryHeartMe Writeup

content: Professional Experience#Nov 2025Cybersecurity Training ParticipantSecurity Manpower Training (SMT)Participated in the National Cybersecurity Training Initiative organized by INKOR and NSHC.Completed intensive Capture the Flag (CTF) and hands-on cybersecurity modules covering network & web attacks, malware analysis, and reverse engineering.Selected for onsite advanced training in South Korea, which included malware analysis workshops, offensive security exercises, and company visits to cybersecurity firms.Sep 2024 – Dec 2024Network System EngineerMetrodata Academy InternshipAssisted in deploying, troubleshooting, and configuring network security solutions, including Palo Alto Next-Generation Firewall, Cortex XDR, Exabeam SIEM, and F5 Load Balancer.Collaborated with 5+ clients during Proof of Concept (PoC) sessions, providing technical support and presenting solution designs.Gained experience in project documentation, User Acceptance Testing (UAT), and proposal creation for enterprise clients.Education#2025Bachelor of Computer ScienceUniversitas Ciputra SurabayaGPA: 3.88 / 4.00Award: 50% ScholarshipRelevant Coursework: Machine Learning, AI, Cyber Security, Computer Networks, Software Engineering, OOP, Mobile Development, Website Development, Database.Feb 2024 – June 2024Student Exchange ProgramDongseo University, South KoreaMajor: Bachelor of Computer EngineeringGPA: 4.25 / 4.5Award: Fully-Funded GKS Scholarship (Korean Scholarship)Relevant Coursework: Data Science, AR & VR Programming, Korean Language, Web Server Programming.
